title: Transfusion Therapy and Biology
authors:
Pierre Tiberghien, Olivier Garraud,

Jacques Chiaroni

items.subtopic: Transfusion Therapy and Biology | BLOOD COMPONENTS
items.title: Leukocyte-Reduced Blood Components Reduce Adverse Reactions
items.content: Most blood components are now leukocyte-reduced to minimize adverse reactions. This reduction is associated with a decreased incidence of febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reactions (FNHTRs), infections from intracellular pathogens like cytomegalovirus, alloimmunization, and immunomodulation.
keyPoints: Leukocyte-reduced blood components contain <1–5.10^6 donor leukocytes.
Reduction in FNHTRs, CMV infections, alloimmunization, and immunomodulation.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · Transfusion Therapy and Biology | BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.subtopic: Transfusion Therapy and Biology | BLOOD COMPONENTS
items.title: Adverse Reactions from Blood Components and Preventive Measures
items.content: Blood components can cause adverse reactions such as fainting and iron deficiency. Preventive measures and appropriate treatments are necessary to manage these reactions.
keyPoints: Common adverse reactions include fainting and iron deficiency.
Preventive approaches and treatment strategies are essential.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · Transfusion Therapy and Biology | BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.title: Understanding Alloimmunization and Its Clinical Implications
items.content: Alloimmunization occurs when transfusion introduces allogeneic determinants, leading to the production of alloantibodies. These antibodies can target red blood cells, HLA, platelet antigens, or neutrophil antigens, causing complications such as hemolysis, fever, and transfusion refractoriness.
keyPoints: Alloimmunization results in anti-RBC, anti-HLA, anti-HPA, and anti-HNA antibodies.
Anti-RBC immunization can cause hemolysis.
Anti-HLA or anti-HPA Abs may lead to fever and platelet transfusion refractoriness.
Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI) is a severe complication from donor anti-HLA or anti-HNA antibodies.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 1)

items.title: The Role of Blood Group Antigens in Disease Susceptibility
items.content: Blood group antigens, expressed on red blood cells and other cells, have immunizing potential during transfusion, pregnancy, or transplantation. They also interact with the environment and pathogens, influencing disease susceptibility, such as reduced severity of malaria in type O individuals.
keyPoints: ~390 different blood groups are described within ~45 systems.
Blood group antigens can influence individual susceptibilities to diseases like malaria.
Type O individuals have less severe malaria compared to non-O patients.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 1)

items.title: Importance of Rh System in Transfusion Compatibility
items.content: The Rh system, particularly the D antigen, is highly immunogenic and critical for transfusion compatibility. The presence or absence of the D antigen determines Rh positivity or negativity, with significant implications for transfusion practices.
keyPoints: The Rh system includes nearly 56 antigens, with D being the most immunogenic (RH1).
D antigen is present in 85% of Caucasians, 93% of Africans, and >99% of Asians.
Routine testing for five RBC group systems ensures 95% transfusion compatibility.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 1)

items.title: Kell Antigen Prevalence and Clinical Implications
items.content: The Kell antigen system includes the K antigen, which is significant due to its immunogenicity. The prevalence of K positivity varies by ancestry, with notable clinical implications for transfusion reactions.
keyPoints: 9% of Caucasian individuals are K positive (KEL:1), while 2% of African ancestry individuals are K positive.
91% of Caucasians and 98% of Africans are K negative (KEL:-1).
Immunogenicity ranking: ABO > Rh > Kell.
Rare absence of Kx protein leads to McLeod phenotype, characterized by acanthocytosis, shortened RBC survival, and progressive muscular dystrophy with cardiac defects.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 2)

items.title: Duffy Antigen System and Its Clinical Relevance
items.content: The Duffy antigen system is crucial for understanding transfusion reactions, especially in individuals of African ancestry due to the high prevalence of the Fy(a-b-) phenotype.
keyPoints: Fy(a-b-) phenotype exclusive (with exceptions) to individuals of African ancestry, with frequencies 70–100% depending on population.
Linked to a double dose of silent FY^0 allele.
These individuals may develop antibodies against high-frequency antigens FY3 and FY5 after transfusion or pregnancy.
Associated with low granulocyte count but not linked to disease.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 2)

items.title: Kidd Antigen System and Its Clinical Implications
items.content: The Kidd antigen system includes the Jk(a-b-) phenotype, which is significant in Polynesian populations due to its association with antibody development post-transfusion or pregnancy.
keyPoints: Jk(a-b-) phenotype found in Polynesian populations.
Linked to a double dose of silent J^k0 allele.
Individuals may develop antibodies against high-frequency anti-JK3 antigen after transfusion or pregnancy.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: RARE RBC PHENOTYPES | CLINICAL INDICATIONS AND EFFICACY ASSESSMENT OF BLOOD COMPONENTS | ADVERSE REACTIONS TO BLOOD COMPONENTS
items.title: Management of Patients with Rare RBC Phenotypes
items.content: Patients with rare RBC phenotypes, such as those lacking common antigens or possessing private antigens, pose significant transfusion challenges. These patients often require blood from specialized programs that can provide rare blood types, sometimes necessitating cryopreserved blood components.
keyPoints: Public-negative individuals are difficult to transfuse with conventional resources.
Designated blood banks and rare blood programs are essential for these patients.
Targeting specific ethnic populations may be necessary due to genotype specificities (e.g., Bombay phenotype in southwestern Indians).
Hemoglobinopathies like sickle cell disease are more common in African ancestry.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · RARE RBC PHENOTYPES | CLINICAL INDICATIONS AND EFFICACY ASSESSMENT OF BLOOD COMPONENTS | ADVERSE REACTIONS TO BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.subtopic: RARE RBC PHENOTYPES | CLINICAL INDICATIONS AND EFFICACY ASSESSMENT OF BLOOD COMPONENTS | ADVERSE REACTIONS TO BLOOD COMPONENTS
items.title: Adverse Reactions to Blood Components and Their Management
items.content: Transfusion of blood components can lead to adverse reactions, both immune-mediated and nonimmune. Monitoring for warning signs is crucial, and transfusions should be stopped if an adverse reaction is suspected. The FDA reported 37 transfusion-associated fatalities annually between 2017-2021, with TACO being the most common cause.
keyPoints: Adverse reactions can be immune or nonimmune in origin.
Common causes of death include TACO (32%), TRALI (21%), hemolysis (21%), and microbial contamination (13%).
Approximately 37 transfusion-associated fatalities were reported yearly to the FDA from 2017-2021.
Transfusion should be stopped if an adverse reaction is suspected, with supportive care initiated as needed.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · RARE RBC PHENOTYPES | CLINICAL INDICATIONS AND EFFICACY ASSESSMENT OF BLOOD COMPONENTS | ADVERSE REACTIONS TO BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.title: Preventing Hemolytic Transfusion Reactions
items.content: Prevention of hemolytic transfusion reactions relies on meticulous pretransfusion testing, including ABO and Rh phenotyping, to ensure compatibility between donor blood components and the recipient. Special attention is required for patients with a history of alloimmunization or those receiving monoclonal antibody treatments that may interfere with screening.
keyPoints: Pretransfusion testing includes ABO D phenotype determination and typing for Rh Ags (CcEe), Kell, Duffy, Kidd, and MNS systems.
Molecular typing is increasingly used to predict RBC phenotypes.
Double ABO determination can be considered in the absence of systematic crossmatch.
Screening for alloAbs involves mixing patient serum with type O RBCs expressing known Ags.
Special care needed for patients on monoclonal Ab treatments; sample dithiothreitol pretreatment may offset interference.
Crossmatching is crucial, especially for frequently transfused or alloimmunized recipients.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Managing ABO Incompatibility in Platelet Transfusions
items.content: ABO plasmatic incompatibility can occur during platelet transfusions due to the presence of plasma with high-titer anti-A/B antibodies. To mitigate this risk, it is preferable to use platelets from donors with low-titer anti-A/B only when ABO-identical PCs are not feasible.
keyPoints: ABO plasmatic incompatibility primarily occurs during PC transfusions due to the plasma volume (100–300 mL).
High-titer anti-A/B Abs can induce hemolytic reactions; a threshold titer of 1/64 may be appropriate for defining high titers.
Use of additive solutions in PCs reduces the risk of plasmatic incompatibility.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Severity of Acute Hemolytic Reactions
items.content: Acute hemolytic reactions can present with symptoms such as hypotension, tachypnea, fever, chills, chest/back pain, hemoglobinuria, and hemoglobinemia. In severe cases, complications like DIC, acute renal failure, shock, or death may occur.
keyPoints: Symptoms include hypotension, tachypnea, tachycardia, fever (+1–2°C), chills, chest/back pain, hemoglobinuria, and hemoglobinemia.
Severe cases can lead to DIC, acute renal failure, shock, or death.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis of Delayed Hemolytic Transfusion Reactions
items.content: Delayed hemolytic reactions occur due to anamnestic responses in previously sensitized patients with low alloantibody levels. Diagnosis is based on clinical and laboratory findings.
keyPoints: Clinical manifestations include icterus and persisting or worsening anemia.
Alloantibodies become detectable 1–2 weeks post-transfusion.
Diagnostic criteria: persistent/worsening anemia, depleted haptoglobin levels, hemoglobinemia/hemoglobinuria, elevated lactate dehydrogenase and unconjugated bilirubin.
Direct antiglobulin test (DAT) is usually positive; indirect antiglobulin test (IAT) may also be positive.
Ab elution from RBCs can identify the culprit alloantibody.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 2)

items.title: Management of Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reactions
items.content: Immediate management focuses on supportive care and preventing further complications.
keyPoints: Promptly interrupt the transfusion upon suspicion of a reaction.
Conduct biological workup and clerical checks to prevent repeat errors.
Administer isotonic saline and diuretics to maintain urine output.
Consider forced alkaline diuresis, electrolyte correction, and pressor support if needed.
In cases of DIC/severe bleeding, provide platelets, plasma, cryoprecipitate, or fibrinogen.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 2)

items.title: Prevention and Management of Hemolysis in Allogeneic Transplantation
items.content: Minor ABO incompatibility and passenger lymphocyte syndrome can cause hemolysis post-transplant.
keyPoints: Minor ABO incompatibility is a common cause of hemolysis after allogeneic transplantation.
Passenger lymphocytes may produce alloantibodies causing hemolysis 5–14 days post-transplant.
Reduced-intensity conditioning and cyclosporine increase risk; GVHD prophylaxis targeting B cells (e.g., methotrexate) reduces incidence.
Transfuse RBCs compatible with graft donor to minimize risk.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 2)

items.title: Febrile Nonhemolytic Transfusion Reaction: Incidence and Prevention
items.content: FNHTR is the most common transfusion reaction, occurring in up to 300 per 10^5 blood components. It presents with chills, rigors, and a temperature rise of ≥1°C due to proinflammatory cytokines or recipient antibodies against donor antigens.
keyPoints: Incidence: Up to 300 per 10^5 BCs
Symptoms: Chills, rigors, ≥1°C rise in body temperature
Cause: Proinflammatory cytokines or recipient antibodies
Diagnosis: Exclude other causes like infection and hemolysis
Prevention: Leukocyte reduction (especially prestorage) and use of additive solutions
Premedication with anti-pyretics is generally ineffective
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 3)

items.title: Allergic Reactions: Severity, Frequency, and Management
items.content: Allergic transfusion reactions range from mild (rash, pruritus) to severe anaphylaxis. Mild reactions occur in ~100 per 10^5 BCs, while severe ones are ~5 per 10^5 BCs.
keyPoints: Mild reaction frequency: ~100 per 10^5 BCs
Severe reaction frequency: ~5 per 10^5 BCs
Symptoms: Rash, pruritus, urticaria, edema; severe cases may include bronchospasm and shock
Cause: Plasma proteins in transfused components
Mild treatment: Stop transfusion, administer antihistamines
Severe treatment: Stop transfusion, maintain vascular access, administer adrenaline (0.3–0.5 mg subcutaneously), possibly steroids, antihistamines, and bronchodilators
Precaution for IgA-deficient patients: Use IgA-deficient plasma and washed cellular BCs
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 3)

items.title: Graft-Versus-Host Disease: Incidence, Risk Factors, and Prevention
items.content: GVHD is a rare but fatal transfusion reaction occurring in severely immunosuppressed patients or those with shared HLA haplotypes. It presents 5–10 days post-transfusion with cytopenia, fever, rash, diarrhea, and liver abnormalities.
keyPoints: Incidence: Extremely rare
Symptoms: Cytopenia, fever, skin rash, diarrhea, liver function abnormalities (5-10 days post-transfusion)
Cause: Engrafted donor T lymphocytes in immunocompromised recipients
Risk factors: Severe immune deficiency, shared HLA haplotypes, immunosuppressive treatments
Treatment resistance: Highly resistant to immunosuppressive and ablative therapies
Prevention: Irradiate cellular BCs (minimum of 25 Gy) or use pathogen reduction technology
At-risk patients: Immune-deficient, undergoing transplantation, treated with immunosuppressants, fetuses receiving intrauterine transfusions, recipients of related donor BCs
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 3)

items.title: Understanding TRALI: Mechanisms and Management
items.content: Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury (TRALI) is a serious complication characterized by hypoxia and noncardiogenic pulmonary edema, typically occurring during or within 6 hours of transfusion. It may be difficult to distinguish from other causes of hypoxia like circulatory overload.
keyPoints: TRALI frequency ranges from 0.5 to 10 per $10^5$ blood components (BCs).
Caused by donor plasma containing high-titer anti-HLA class II antibodies or cytokines/chemokines without HLA interaction.
Leukocytes aggregate in pulmonary vasculature, releasing inflammatory mediators.
Risk reduced by using plasma and platelet concentrates from male donors or non-anti-HLA Abs female donors.
Supportive treatment only; no specific therapy.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 4)

items.title: Posttransfusion Purpura: Identification and Management
items.content: Posttransfusion purpura is a rare thrombocytopenia-related bleeding disorder occurring 5–12 days post-transfusion, predominantly in women. It involves anti-HPA-1a antibodies in HPA-1a-negative individuals.
keyPoints: Incidence of ~1 per $10^5$ BCs.
Characterized by thrombocytopenia and bleeding disorder.
Management includes supportive care, polyvalent immunoglobulin, glucocorticoids, or plasma exchange.
Avoid additional platelet transfusions as they may worsen the condition.
Prevention involves using washed blood components or HPA-compatible donors.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 4)

items.title: Alloimmunization and Platelet Refractoriness: Clinical Implications
items.content: Alloimmunization can lead to platelet refractoriness, where recipients develop antibodies against transfused blood components. This affects the efficacy of platelet transfusions.
keyPoints: Occurs with a frequency of ~100 per $10^5$ red blood cell component (RBCC) transfusions.
Anti-HLA antibodies are common in alloimmunization to leukocytes and platelets.
HLA-compatible PCs should be used if available; otherwise, consider repeated PCs at shortened intervals.
Leukocyte-reduced components reduce immunization incidence.
Recipient factors like fever, splenomegaly, and certain medications can contribute to refractoriness.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 4)

items.title: Understanding TACO: Recognition and Management
items.content: Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overload (TACO) is a prevalent transfusion adverse reaction, now the leading cause of death from transfusions due to reduced TRALI risk. It occurs in patients with certain risk factors like older age or renal failure, presenting with dyspnea, hypoxia, and bilateral alveolar infiltrates on chest x-ray.
keyPoints: Estimated frequency: ~10 to 100 per 10^5 blood components (BCs)
Risk factors: older age, renal failure, preexisting fluid overload, cardiac dysfunction
Clinical presentation: dyspnea, hypoxia, bilateral alveolar infiltrates, systolic hypertension, elevated brain natriuretic peptide
Prevention: identify at-risk patients, slow transfusion rate (1 RBCC over 3–4 h), use diuretics in stable patients with TACO history
Treatment: stop transfusion, administer oxygen and diuretics
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · NONIMMUNOLOGIC TRANSFUSION ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.title: Managing Massive Transfusion Reactions: Citrate Toxicity and More
items.content: Massive transfusions can lead to adverse reactions such as citrate toxicity, hypothermia, hyperkalemia, and dilutional coagulopathy. These occur with the rapid transfusion of large volumes of blood components, leading to complications like hypocalcemia and cardiac dysrhythmias.
keyPoints: Massive transfusion: 50% of patient's total blood volume over 3 h or >5–10 units of RBCCs
Citrate toxicity: chelates calcium, may require calcium infusion
Hypothermia prevention: use inline warmer for BCs at 4°C
Hyperkalemia risk: neonates and patients with renal failure; treatment includes insulin, glucose, calcium gluconate, furosemide
Prevention: use washed or plasma-reduced RBCCs, storage age <7–10 days, avoid RBCCs stored >24 h after irradiation
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · NONIMMUNOLOGIC TRANSFUSION ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.title: Iron Overload in Frequent Transfusions: Monitoring and Management
items.content: Repeated transfusions can lead to iron overload, affecting endocrine, hepatic, and cardiac function. Each red blood cell component (RBCC) contains 200–250 mg of iron, necessitating careful monitoring and management with iron chelation.
keyPoints: Iron content per RBCC: 200–250 mg
Consequences: affects endocrine, hepatic, and cardiac function; potential death from cardiac failure or arrhythmia
Assessment methods: serum ferritin measurements, MRI, liver biopsy
Management: careful monitoring, iron chelation
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · NONIMMUNOLOGIC TRANSFUSION ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.title: Bacterial Contamination in Blood Components: Risks and Management
items.content: Blood components stored at room temperature are susceptible to bacterial proliferation, which can lead to transfusion-transmitted infections. Gram-negative bacteria like Yersinia can grow even at 4°C, posing a risk with red blood cell concentrates (RBCC). Symptoms such as fever and chills may occur during or shortly after transfusion, potentially progressing to severe conditions like septic shock or disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). Immediate action involves sampling for bacterial culture followed by the initiation of broad-spectrum antibiotics.
keyPoints: Bacterial proliferation is a risk in PCs stored at room temperature and RBCCs due to bacteria such as Yersinia that can grow at 4°C.
Symptoms include abrupt fever and chills during or up to several hours after transfusion, with potential progression to septic shock, DIC, and death.
Endotoxin formation within the blood component may contribute to infections.
After sampling for bacterial culture, broad-spectrum antibiotics should be promptly initiated.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · INFECTIOUS ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.title: Emerging Infectious Risks and Surveillance in Transfusion Medicine
items.content: Despite the rarity of transfusion-transmitted infections due to improved screening, new or previously unidentified infectious risks can emerge, as seen with West Nile virus and babesiosis in 2000 in the U.S., and hepatitis E in Europe in 2010. Active surveillance programs are essential for early detection and implementation of mitigation measures such as additional testing and pathogen reduction technologies. Factors influencing transfusion-transmitted infections include local epidemiology, donor deferral rules, and recipient immune status.
keyPoints: Emerging infectious risks like West Nile virus, babesiosis, and hepatitis E highlight the need for active surveillance.
Pathogen reduction technology is effective against many pathogens but not bacterial spores or nonenveloped viruses such as hepatitis A, parvovirus B19, and hepatitis E.
Factors affecting transfusion-transmitted infections include local epidemiology, donor deferral rules, risk reduction measures, storage conditions, recipient immune status, and ongoing treatments.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · INFECTIOUS ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.title: Transfusion-Sparing Strategies: Why Consider Alternatives?
items.content: Patient blood management programs emphasize strategies to reduce the need for transfusions, focusing on addressing iron deficiency pre-surgery and minimizing blood loss. Antifibrinolytic agents like tranexamic acid (TXA) are effective in preventing bleeding complications across various hemorrhage types.
keyPoints: Iron deficiency treatment before surgery can minimize the need for transfusions.
Tranexamic acid (TXA) is effective in preventing bleeding complications.
Erythropoietin stimulates erythrocyte production in anemia from chronic renal failure and other conditions.
Thrombopoietin receptor agonists may reduce platelet transfusion needs due to chemotherapy-induced thrombocytopenia.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · ALTERNATIVES AND PERSPECTIVES

items.title: Emerging Therapies: How They Reduce Transfusion Needs?
items.content: Advanced therapies such as bone marrow transplantation, gene therapy (including CRISPR-Cas9-mediated editing), and stem cell-derived blood cells are reducing transfusion needs in patients with conditions like sickle cell disease or major thalassemia. These approaches offer promising alternatives to traditional transfusions.
keyPoints: Bone marrow transplantation and gene therapy reduce transfusion needs for sickle cell or major thalassemia.
CRISPR-Cas9-mediated gene editing is a notable gene therapy approach.
Stem cell-derived blood cells may become an alternative to rare blood donors.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · ALTERNATIVES AND PERSPECTIVES

items.subtopic: Transfusion Therapy and Biology | BLOOD COMPONENTS
items.question: Which blood component is primarily used to prevent or treat anemia?
items.explanation: Red blood cell concentrates are primarily used to prevent or treat anemia.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · Transfusion Therapy and Biology | BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.subtopic: Transfusion Therapy and Biology | BLOOD COMPONENTS
items.question: What is a benefit of using leukocyte-reduced blood components?
options: Increased risk of febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reactions (FNHTRs)
Reduced incidence of infections with intracellular pathogens like CMV
Higher levels of donor antibodies in the recipient
items.explanation: Leukocyte-reduced blood components are associated with a reduced incidence of infections with intracellular pathogens such as cytomegalovirus (CMV).
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · Transfusion Therapy and Biology | BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.subtopic: Transfusion Therapy and Biology | BLOOD COMPONENTS
items.question: Which processing step is used to prevent graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) in immunosuppressed patients?
items.explanation: Irradiation is used to prevent graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) in immunosuppressed patients.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · Transfusion Therapy and Biology | BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.question: Which immunoglobulin classes can anti-red cell antigens belong to?
items.explanation: Anti-red cell antigens may be of the IgM or IgG immunoglobulin classes.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 1)

items.question: What is a potential complication from anti-HLA or anti-HPA antibodies?
items.explanation: Anti-HLA or anti-HPA antibodies may result in fever and platelet transfusion refractoriness.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 1)

items.question: Which blood group system is most immunogenic within the Rh system?
items.explanation: The D antigen (RH1) of the Rh system is the most immunogenic.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 1)

items.question: Which blood group antigens are expressed on endothelial cells and considered 'tissue' groups?
items.explanation: The ABO antigens, expressed on endothelial cells, are considered 'tissue' groups.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 1)

items.question: Which blood group system is associated with a phenotype that can lead to a progressive form of muscular dystrophy including cardiac defects?
items.explanation: The Kell system's rare absence of the Kx protein is associated with the McLeod phenotype, which includes a progressive form of muscular dystrophy and cardiac defects.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 2)

items.question: Which blood group antigen serves as a receptor for Plasmodium vivax?
items.explanation: The Duffy system's $ Fy^a $ and $ Fy^b $ antigens serve as receptors for Plasmodium vivax.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 2)

items.question: Which blood group phenotype is exclusive to individuals of African ancestry with frequencies reaching up to 100%?
items.explanation: The Duffy system's $ Fy(a-b-) $ phenotype is exclusive to individuals of African ancestry with high frequencies.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 2)

items.question: Which blood group system includes antigens encoded by the GYPA and GYPB genes?
items.explanation: The MNS system includes antigens encoded by the GYPA and GYPB genes.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 2)

items.question: Which population may require targeted blood donation due to specific genotype particularities?
options: Southwestern Indians with the Bombay phenotype
Northern Europeans with high frequency of Rh-negative phenotypes
East Asians with increased incidence of G6PD deficiency
Africans with common sickle cell trait
items.explanation: The passage mentions that specific ethnic populations, such as the Bombay group in southwestern Indians, may require targeted blood donation due to genotype specificities.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · RARE RBC PHENOTYPES | CLINICAL INDICATIONS AND EFFICACY ASSESSMENT OF BLOOD COMPONENTS | ADVERSE REACTIONS TO BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.question: What is the most common cause of transfusion-associated fatalities reported to the FDA between 2017 and 2021?
items.explanation: The passage states that the most frequent cause of death was transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO), accounting for 32%.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · RARE RBC PHENOTYPES | CLINICAL INDICATIONS AND EFFICACY ASSESSMENT OF BLOOD COMPONENTS | ADVERSE REACTIONS TO BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.subtopic: RARE RBC PHENOTYPES | CLINICAL INDICATIONS AND EFFICACY ASSESSMENT OF BLOOD COMPONENTS | ADVERSE REACTIONS TO BLOOD COMPONENTS
items.question: What is a key responsibility of designated blood banks that manage rare blood programs?
options: To provide universal donor blood to all patients
To collect and identify blood from donors with uncommon antigen displays
To ensure cryopreservation of all collected blood components
To minimize the use of blood components in transfusions
items.explanation: The passage highlights that designated blood banks must identify and collect blood from donors exhibiting particular antigen displays on their RBCs or platelets.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · RARE RBC PHENOTYPES | CLINICAL INDICATIONS AND EFFICACY ASSESSMENT OF BLOOD COMPONENTS | ADVERSE REACTIONS TO BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.question: Which antibodies are most commonly responsible for severe hemolytic transfusion reactions?
items.explanation: The passage states that the anti-A or anti-B antibodies are responsible for most of the severe reactions.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is a common cause of dramatic hemolytic transfusion reactions?
items.explanation: Dramatic hemolytic reactions are usually caused by a failure in product or patient identification, erroneous blood grouping, or unidentified anti-RBC alloimmunization.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is the estimated frequency of acute hemolytic adverse reactions per 100,000 transfused blood components?
items.explanation: The passage states that the estimated frequency of acute hemolytic adverse reactions is 1–10 per 100,000 transfused blood components.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 1)

items.question: What should be considered when selecting a compatible blood component for D-negative patients?
items.explanation: Every attempt must be made to provide Rh-negative RBCC to prevent anti-D alloimmunization in D-negative patients.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is the most likely time frame for detecting an alloantibody after a transfusion in delayed hemolytic reactions?
items.explanation: Alloantibodies are detectable 1-2 weeks after the transfusion in delayed hemolytic reactions.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which test is used to detect immunoglobulin and possibly complement on the surface of a recipient's RBCs?
items.explanation: The direct antiglobulin test (DAT, or direct Coombs test) detects immunoglobulin and possibly complement on the surface of RBCs.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 2)

items.question: What is a common cause of clinically significant hemolysis after allogeneic hematopoietic transplantation?
items.explanation: Minor ABO incompatibility and passenger lymphocytes are common causes of clinically significant hemolysis after allogeneic hematopoietic transplantation.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which prophylactic treatment is used to prevent or minimize acute and delayed hemolysis when transfusion of incompatible RBCs is unavoidable?
items.explanation: Prophylaxis with steroids (hydrocortisone) and polyvalent immunoglobulin is used to prevent or minimize acute and delayed hemolysis.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which intervention is most effective in preventing Febrile Nonhemolytic Transfusion Reactions (FNHTR)?
options: Stopping the transfusion and administering antihistamines
items.explanation: Leukocyte reduction, especially prestorage, can prevent FNHTR.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 3)

items.question: What is the most common treatment for mild allergic transfusion reactions?
options: Stopping the transfusion and administering antihistamines
items.explanation: Mild allergic reactions may be treated by temporarily stopping the transfusion and administering antihistamine drugs.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 3)

items.question: Which patients are at risk for anaphylactic reactions due to IgA deficiency?
options: Patients with a history of allergic transfusion reaction
items.explanation: Patients who are IgA deficient may be at risk of anaphylactic reactions associated with plasma transfusion.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 3)

items.question: What is the primary method to prevent Transfusion-Associated Graft-Versus-Host Disease (GVHD) in at-risk patients?
items.explanation: Prevention in at-risk patients relies on the irradiation of cellular BCs.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury (TRALI)?
options: Noncardiogenic pulmonary edema with bilateral interstitial infiltrates on chest x-ray during or within 6 hours after transfusion.
Cardiogenic pulmonary edema with unilateral infiltrates on chest x-ray occurring more than 72 hours post-transfusion.
Hypoxia due to circulatory overload without changes in chest x-ray findings.
Immediate onset of symptoms within minutes of transfusion.
items.explanation: TRALI is characterized by noncardiogenic pulmonary edema with bilateral interstitial infiltrates on chest x-ray during or within 6 hours after transfusion.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 4)

items.question: What is the primary cause of TRALI?
options: Transfusion of donor plasma containing high-titer anti-HLA class II antibodies.
Presence of bacterial contamination in the transfused blood components.
Circulatory overload due to excessive volume transfusion.
Immediate hypersensitivity reaction mediated by IgE.
items.explanation: TRALI usually results from the transfusion of donor plasma that contains high-titer anti-HLA class II antibodies.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 4)

items.question: Which factor is NOT associated with an increased risk of TRALI?
items.explanation: The transfusion of plasma and PCs from male donors and nulliparous or parous female donors without anti-HLA Abs has significantly reduced the risk of TRALI.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 4)

items.question: What is a common management strategy for Posttransfusion Purpura?
options: Use of washed blood components or HPA-compatible donors
items.explanation: Prevention of recurrence includes use of washed BCs or BCs from HPA-compatible donors.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 4)

items.question: Which of the following is a common risk factor for Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overload (TACO)?
items.explanation: Preexisting fluid overload is a risk factor for TACO.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · NONIMMUNOLOGIC TRANSFUSION ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.question: What is the primary cause of death from transfusion after mitigating TRALI risk?
items.explanation: TACO is now the main cause of death from transfusion since TRALI risk has been mitigated.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · NONIMMUNOLOGIC TRANSFUSION ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.question: Which intervention can prevent hypothermia during rapid transfusion of blood components?
items.explanation: Use of an inline warmer will prevent hypothermia during rapid transfusion.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · NONIMMUNOLOGIC TRANSFUSION ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.question: Which blood component is associated with iron overload in frequently transfused recipients?
items.explanation: Iron overload can occur due to the iron content in each Red Blood Cell (RBC) unit.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · NONIMMUNOLOGIC TRANSFUSION ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.question: Which of the following pathogens is most likely to grow at 4°C and cause infections related to RBCC transfusion?
items.explanation: Yersinia can grow at 4°C and may be implicated in infections related to RBCC transfusion.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · INFECTIOUS ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.question: What is the recommended immediate action after sampling for bacterial culture in a recipient of contaminated blood components?
options: Wait for culture results before starting treatment
items.explanation: Broad-spectrum antibiotics should be promptly initiated after sampling for bacterial culture.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · INFECTIOUS ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT effectively reduced by pathogen reduction processes in blood components?
items.explanation: Pathogen reduction processes are most often ineffective for bacterial spores and nonenveloped viruses such as hepatitis A virus, parvovirus B19.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · INFECTIOUS ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.question: Which agent is effective in preventing bleeding complications across various forms of hemorrhage?
items.explanation: Antifibrinolytic agents such as tranexamic acid (TXA) are effective in preventing bleeding complications.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · ALTERNATIVES AND PERSPECTIVES

items.question: What is a strategy to reduce platelet transfusion needs resulting from chemotherapy-induced thrombocytopenia?
options: Treatment of iron deficiency before surgery
items.explanation: Thrombopoietin receptor agonists may reduce platelet transfusion needs in chemotherapy-induced thrombocytopenia.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · ALTERNATIVES AND PERSPECTIVES

items.question: Which of the following approaches can dramatically reduce transfusion needs in patients with sickle cell or major thalassemia?
options: Bone marrow transplantation and gene therapy
items.explanation: Bone marrow transplantation and gene therapy approaches, including CRISPR-Cas9-mediated gene editing, reduce transfusion needs in sickle cell or major thalassemia.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · ALTERNATIVES AND PERSPECTIVES

items.subtopic: Transfusion Therapy and Biology | BLOOD COMPONENTS
items.statement: Autologous blood components are now common practice due to their significant advantages over homologous BCs.
items.explanation: Autologous BCs are exceptional as they present little to no advantage over homologous BCs.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · Transfusion Therapy and Biology | BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.subtopic: Transfusion Therapy and Biology | BLOOD COMPONENTS
items.statement: Universal prestorage leukocyte reduction is recommended for most blood components.
items.explanation: Universal prestorage leukocyte reduction has been recommended to reduce adverse reactions.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · Transfusion Therapy and Biology | BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.statement: Anti-RBC antibodies can lead to transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI).
items.explanation: TRALI is associated with anti-HLA or anti-HNA immunization in the donor, not anti-RBC antibodies.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 1)

items.statement: The ABO blood group antigens are specific to humans and not shared by other species.
items.explanation: ABO antigens are shared by many species including viruses and bacteria.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 1)

items.statement: Individuals with the McLeod phenotype may develop antibodies against high-frequency antigens after transfusion.
items.explanation: The passage states that individuals with the rare absence of Kx protein in the Kell system, associated with the McLeod phenotype, can develop antibodies after transfusion.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 2)

items.statement: The $ J^{k}(a-b-) $ phenotype is found exclusively in individuals of African ancestry.
items.explanation: The $ J^{k}(a-b-) $ phenotype is found in Polynesian populations, not exclusively in individuals of African ancestry.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: RARE RBC PHENOTYPES | CLINICAL INDICATIONS AND EFFICACY ASSESSMENT OF BLOOD COMPONENTS | ADVERSE REACTIONS TO BLOOD COMPONENTS
items.statement: Adverse reactions to blood components are always life-threatening.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that most adverse reactions are non-life-threatening, although serious reactions can occur.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · RARE RBC PHENOTYPES | CLINICAL INDICATIONS AND EFFICACY ASSESSMENT OF BLOOD COMPONENTS | ADVERSE REACTIONS TO BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.subtopic: RARE RBC PHENOTYPES | CLINICAL INDICATIONS AND EFFICACY ASSESSMENT OF BLOOD COMPONENTS | ADVERSE REACTIONS TO BLOOD COMPONENTS
items.statement: Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO) was the leading cause of transfusion-related fatalities from 2017 to 2021.
items.explanation: The passage states that TACO accounted for 32% of reported transfusion-associated fatalities.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · RARE RBC PHENOTYPES | CLINICAL INDICATIONS AND EFFICACY ASSESSMENT OF BLOOD COMPONENTS | ADVERSE REACTIONS TO BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.statement: Hemolysis can occur after transfusion of blood components containing incompatible plasma with a large amount of alloAbs.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that hemolysis may occur upon transfusion of blood components containing incompatible plasma with a large amount of alloAbs directed against the recipient's RBCs.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Acute hemolytic reactions are always fatal.
items.explanation: The passage states that severe reactions can be fatal but does not say all acute hemolytic reactions are always fatal.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Nonimmune mechanisms of transfusion-associated hemolysis include thermal, osmotic, and mechanical mechanisms.
items.explanation: Thermal, osmotic, and mechanical are nonimmune mechanisms that can cause transfusion-associated hemolysis.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions always require a transfusion of incompatible RBCs.
items.explanation: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions can occur with compatible or incompatible RBCs, but are more severe with incompatible ones.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Premedication with anti-pyretics is effective in decreasing FNHTR.
items.explanation: Premedication with anti-pyretics has generally proven ineffective at decreasing the rate of FNHTR.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 3)

items.statement: Graft-Versus-Host Disease is a frequent complication after allogeneic hematopoietic transplantation.
items.explanation: GVHD is a frequent complication of allogeneic hematopoietic transplantation.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 3)

items.statement: TRALI can be effectively treated with specific medications targeting the underlying cause.
items.explanation: Treatment for TRALI is supportive only, as there are no specific medications to target its underlying cause.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 4)

items.statement: Posttransfusion Purpura typically occurs within a few hours after transfusion.
items.explanation: Posttransfusion Purpura develops 5–12 days after PC transfusion.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 4)

items.statement: Hypotensive reactions are defined by an abrupt increase in blood pressure of >30 mmHg.
items.explanation: Hypotensive reactions involve a drop, not an increase, in blood pressure.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · NONIMMUNOLOGIC TRANSFUSION ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.statement: Necrotizing enterocolitis has been frequently described with close temporal association to RBC transfusion.
items.explanation: The association of necrotizing enterocolitis with RBC transfusion is infrequent and causality remains uncertain.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · NONIMMUNOLOGIC TRANSFUSION ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.statement: Transfusion-transmitted infections have become increasingly common in recent years.
items.explanation: Transfusion-transmitted infections are increasingly rare.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · INFECTIOUS ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.statement: Pathogen reduction processes can effectively eliminate all types of pathogens from blood components.
items.explanation: These processes are most often ineffective for bacterial spores and nonenveloped viruses.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · INFECTIOUS ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.statement: Transfusion safety considerations now include efficacy aspects.
items.explanation: Issues surrounding transfusion safety now fully encompass transfusion efficacy.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · ALTERNATIVES AND PERSPECTIVES

items.statement: Stem cell-derived blood cells are currently a widely used alternative to rare blood donors.
items.explanation: Stem cell-derived blood cells may in the future become an alternative, but they are not yet widely used.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · ALTERNATIVES AND PERSPECTIVES

items.assertion: Malaria is less severe in type O individuals.
items.reason: Type O blood group antigens are not expressed on RBCs, reducing the severity of malaria.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 1)

items.assertion: All blood group antigens can activate complement.
items.reason: Carbohydrate blood groups like ABO have a major hemolytic capacity by binding and activating the complement cascade.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because not all blood group antigens can activate complement; only specific ones, such as those in the ABO system, do.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 1)

items.assertion: The Duffy system's $ Fy(a-b-) $ phenotype is linked to a silent FY^0 allele.
items.reason: This phenotype results from the presence of a double dose of the silent FY^0 allele.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 2)

items.assertion: The Kell system's immunogenicity is higher than that of the ABO system.
items.reason: Kell antigens are highly immunogenic and can cause significant transfusion reactions.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the Kell system's immunogenicity is third behind the ABO and Rh systems.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · BLOOD GROUP ANTIGENS AND ANTIBODIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: RARE RBC PHENOTYPES | CLINICAL INDICATIONS AND EFFICACY ASSESSMENT OF BLOOD COMPONENTS | ADVERSE REACTIONS TO BLOOD COMPONENTS
items.assertion: Patients with rare RBC phenotypes may face difficulties in finding matching donors.
items.reason: Such patients often require access to designated blood banks that have rare blood programs.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, and the reason correctly explains why these patients face difficulties due to the need for specialized resources.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · RARE RBC PHENOTYPES | CLINICAL INDICATIONS AND EFFICACY ASSESSMENT OF BLOOD COMPONENTS | ADVERSE REACTIONS TO BLOOD COMPONENTS

items.assertion: Pretransfusion testing is essential to prevent hemolytic transfusion reactions.
items.reason: Pretransfusion testing includes determining the ABO D phenotype and additional typing for other main Rh antigens.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why pretransfusion testing is essential.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: D-positive RBCC can be safely transfused to a D-negative patient who lacks anti-D.
items.reason: Such transfusion will always lead to alloimmunization against the D antigen.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because an estimated 20–22% of recipients become alloimmunized after such a transfusion.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Passenger lymphocyte syndrome is a cause of hemolysis after allogeneic hematopoietic transplantation.
items.reason: Viable donor B lymphocytes transferred with the graft may produce alloantibodies targeting recipient red cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Polyvalent immunoglobulin can induce acute hemolysis in patients treated for thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura.
items.reason: Polyvalent immunoglobulin contains high titers of anti-A and/or anti-B antibodies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Allergic transfusion reactions are primarily caused by preformed antibodies.
items.reason: Most of the allergic presentation may not depend on preformed Abs and may be attributable to soluble mediators triggering histamine and serotonin release from platelets and leukocytes.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because most allergic presentations do not depend on preformed antibodies.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 3)

items.assertion: Transfusion-Associated GVHD can be effectively treated with immunosuppressive therapies.
items.reason: Transfusion-associated GVHD is highly resistant to treatment with immunosuppressive therapies.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because Transfusion-Associated GVHD is resistant to such treatments.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 3)

items.assertion: Leukocyte-reduced blood components reduce the risk of TRALI.
items.reason: Leukocytes aggregate in the pulmonary vasculature and release inflammatory mediators, contributing to TRALI.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why leukocyte-reduced blood components reduce the risk of TRALI.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 4)

items.assertion: Alloimmunization to RBC antigens can lead to hemolytic disease of the fetus or newborn.
items.reason: Women sensitized to RBC antigens are at risk if they bear a fetus with compatible antigens.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association between alloimmunization and hemolytic disease of the fetus or newborn.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · IMMUNE-MEDIATED ADVERSE REACTIONS (part 4)

items.assertion: Iron overload can lead to cardiac failure or arrhythmia.
items.reason: Each RBC unit contains 200–250 mg of iron, which accumulates in frequently transfused patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · NONIMMUNOLOGIC TRANSFUSION ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.assertion: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome is caused by rapid correction of chronic severe anemia.
items.reason: The syndrome has been described within 10 days after RBC transfusion, mainly in women with long-standing anemia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · NONIMMUNOLOGIC TRANSFUSION ADVERSE REACTIONS

items.assertion: Linked large-scale databases will be instrumental in assessing transfusion safety and efficacy.
items.reason: Optimal transfusion care requires consideration of new criteria related to donor, blood product, and recipient characteristics.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not the explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Transfusion Therapy and Biology · ALTERNATIVES AND PERSPECTIVES
